Explore Concepts About Make a Difference Useful Links

Vertebrates on Singapore shores: text index

vertebrates text index | photo index   Text index of vertebrates on Singapore shores
currently mainly mangroves, shores, coastal forests with some also found in other wild places.Don’t know the name of the vertebrate? Try the vertebrate photo index.

index to fishes mammals birds [shore
snakes](snakes/snakeindex) other reptiles amphibians  

Mammals   Family Cercopithecidae   Macaca fascicularis (Long-tailed macaque)

  Family Dugongidae   Dugong dugon (Dugong) (CR: Critically Endangered)

  Family Delphinidae   Sousa chinensis (Indo-Pacific hump-backed dolphin) (EN: Endangered)

  Family Emballonuridae   Saccolaimus saccolaimus (Pouched tomb bat)

  Family Mustelidae   Lutrogale perspicillata (Smooth otter) (CR: Critically Endangered)

  Family Pteropodidae   Cynopterus brachyotis (Lesser dog-faced fruit bat)

  Family Sciuridae   Callosciurus notatus (Plantain squirrel)

  Family Suidae   Sus scrofa (Wild boar)

  Family Viverridae   Paradoxurus hermaphroditus (Toddycat or Common palm civet)

Other reptiles

  Family Agamidae   Bronchodela cristatella (Green crested lizard)
Calotes versicolor (Changeable lizard)

  Family Cheloniidae (sea turtles)   Chelonia mydas (Green sea turtle) (CR: Critically Endangered)
Eretmochelys imbricata (Hawksbill turtle) (CR: Critically Endangered)

  Family Crocodylidae   Crocodylus porosus (Estuarine crocodile) (CR: Critically Endangered)

  Family Scincidae   Eutropis multifasciata (Common sun skink)

  Family Varanidae   Varanus salvator (Malayan water monitor)

Amphibians

  Family Dicroglossidae   Fejervarya/Rana crancrivora (Crab-eating frog)

Birds   Shorebirds and seabirds in general

  Family Bucerotidae   Anthracoceros albirostris (Oriental pied-hornbill) (CR: Critically Endangered)

  Family Phasianidae   Gallus gallus (Red junglefowl) (EN: Endangered)

in red are those listed among the threatened plants of Singapore from Davison, G.W. H. and P. K. L. Ng and Ho Hua Chew,

  1. The Singapore Red Data Book: Threatened plants and animals of Singapore.
